title: The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
text:
Artificial intelligence is no longer a concept from science fiction. It is already embedded in our daily lives, from the algorithms that recommend what we watch on streaming platforms to the facial recognition systems used in airports. As AI becomes more powerful, an urgent question arises: how should we regulate this technology to ensure it benefits humanity rather than harms it?

Proponents of AI argue that it has enormous potential to solve some of the world's most pressing problems. In medicine, AI algorithms can detect diseases like cancer at earlier stages than human doctors. In climate science, machine learning models can predict extreme weather events with increasing accuracy. In education, adaptive learning platforms can personalize instruction for each student. The possibilities, they claim, are virtually limitless.

However, critics raise serious concerns. AI systems can perpetuate and even amplify existing biases. For example, facial recognition software has been shown to be significantly less accurate when identifying people with darker skin tones. Automated hiring tools have been found to discriminate against women and minorities. There is also the question of job displacement — as AI automates routine tasks, millions of workers could find themselves unemployed.

Perhaps the most troubling issue is accountability. When an AI system makes a harmful decision — denying someone a loan, misdiagnosing a patient, or causing an accident in a self-driving car — who is responsible? The programmer? The company? The algorithm itself?

Most experts agree that some form of regulation is necessary, but they disagree on what that regulation should look like. Some advocate for strict government oversight, while others prefer industry self-regulation. What is clear is that we cannot afford to ignore the ethical implications of a technology that is reshaping every aspect of our lives.
translation:
La inteligencia artificial ya no es un concepto de ciencia ficción. Ya está integrada en nuestra vida diaria, desde los algoritmos que recomiendan lo que vemos en plataformas de streaming hasta los sistemas de reconocimiento facial usados en aeropuertos. A medida que la IA se vuelve más poderosa, surge una pregunta urgente: ¿cómo deberíamos regular esta tecnología para asegurar que beneficie a la humanidad en lugar de dañarla?

Los defensores de la IA argumentan que tiene un enorme potencial para resolver algunos de los problemas más urgentes del mundo. En medicina, los algoritmos de IA pueden detectar enfermedades como el cáncer en etapas más tempranas que los médicos humanos. En ciencia climática, los modelos de aprendizaje automático pueden predecir eventos climáticos extremos con creciente precisión. En educación, las plataformas de aprendizaje adaptativo pueden personalizar la instrucción para cada estudiante. Las posibilidades, afirman, son virtualmente ilimitadas.

Sin embargo, los críticos plantean preocupaciones serias. Los sistemas de IA pueden perpetuar e incluso amplificar sesgos existentes. Por ejemplo, se ha demostrado que el software de reconocimiento facial es significativamente menos preciso al identificar personas con tonos de piel más oscuros. Se ha descubierto que las herramientas de contratación automatizadas discriminan contra mujeres y minorías. También está la cuestión del desplazamiento laboral — a medida que la IA automatiza tareas rutinarias, millones de trabajadores podrían encontrarse desempleados.

Quizás el problema más preocupante es la responsabilidad. Cuando un sistema de IA toma una decisión dañina — negar un préstamo a alguien, diagnosticar erróneamente a un paciente o causar un accidente en un auto autónomo — ¿quién es responsable? ¿El programador? ¿La empresa? ¿El algoritmo en sí?

La mayoría de los expertos coinciden en que alguna forma de regulación es necesaria, pero no están de acuerdo en cómo debería ser esa regulación. Algunos abogan por una supervisión estricta del gobierno, mientras que otros prefieren la autorregulación de la industria. Lo que está claro es que no podemos permitirnos ignorar las implicaciones éticas de una tecnología que está transformando cada aspecto de nuestras vidas.

questions.question: According to the text, what is one example of AI bias?
options: AI recommends the wrong movies on streaming platforms.
Facial recognition software is less accurate for people with darker skin tones.
AI weather predictions are often wrong.
questions.explanation: The text states that 'facial recognition software has been shown to be significantly less accurate when identifying people with darker skin tones.'

questions.question: What does the author describe as 'perhaps the most troubling issue' with AI?
options: Determining who is responsible when AI causes harm.
The speed at which AI is developing.
questions.explanation: The text says 'Perhaps the most troubling issue is accountability' and asks who is responsible when AI systems make harmful decisions.

questions.question: What is the general consensus among experts about AI regulation?
options: Strict government control is the only option.
Some regulation is necessary, but they disagree on the specifics.
questions.explanation: The text states that 'Most experts agree that some form of regulation is necessary, but they disagree on what that regulation should look like.'

title: Book Review: '1984' by George Orwell
text:
George Orwell's '1984', published in 1949, remains one of the most influential novels of the twentieth century. Set in a dystopian future where the totalitarian Party controls every aspect of life, the novel follows Winston Smith, a low-ranking Party member who secretly harbors rebellious thoughts against the regime led by the mysterious Big Brother.

What makes '1984' so compelling is not its plot, which is relatively straightforward, but its terrifyingly detailed vision of a society built on surveillance, propaganda, and the manipulation of truth. Orwell introduces concepts that have entered everyday language: 'Big Brother' for omnipresent surveillance, 'doublethink' for the ability to hold two contradictory beliefs simultaneously, and 'Newspeak' for language deliberately designed to limit free thought.

The novel's exploration of power is remarkably sophisticated. Orwell understood that authoritarian regimes do not merely suppress dissent — they seek to control reality itself. The Party's slogan, 'Who controls the past controls the future; who controls the present controls the past,' reveals a chilling truth about how history can be weaponized.

Critics have occasionally dismissed '1984' as overly pessimistic or heavy-handed in its political messaging. There is some validity to this criticism; the romantic subplot between Winston and Julia can feel underdeveloped, and the final third of the novel, while powerful, becomes almost unbearably bleak. However, these flaws seem minor when measured against the book's prophetic insights.

In an era of fake news, government surveillance programs, and the erosion of privacy, '1984' feels more relevant than ever. Orwell did not predict the future with precision, but he identified the mechanisms by which freedom can be gradually dismantled. For that reason alone, it deserves to be read — and reread — by every generation.
translation:
La novela '1984' de George Orwell, publicada en 1949, sigue siendo una de las novelas más influyentes del siglo veinte. Ambientada en un futuro distópico donde el Partido totalitario controla cada aspecto de la vida, la novela sigue a Winston Smith, un miembro de bajo rango del Partido que secretamente alberga pensamientos rebeldes contra el régimen liderado por el misterioso Gran Hermano.

Lo que hace a '1984' tan cautivante no es su trama, que es relativamente directa, sino su visión terroríficamente detallada de una sociedad construida sobre la vigilancia, la propaganda y la manipulación de la verdad. Orwell introduce conceptos que han entrado al lenguaje cotidiano: 'Gran Hermano' para la vigilancia omnipresente, 'doblepensar' para la capacidad de sostener dos creencias contradictorias simultáneamente, y 'neolengua' para un lenguaje deliberadamente diseñado para limitar el pensamiento libre.

La exploración del poder en la novela es notablemente sofisticada. Orwell entendió que los regímenes autoritarios no simplemente suprimen la disidencia — buscan controlar la realidad misma. El eslogan del Partido, 'Quien controla el pasado controla el futuro; quien controla el presente controla el pasado,' revela una verdad escalofriante sobre cómo la historia puede ser convertida en arma.

Los críticos ocasionalmente han descartado '1984' como excesivamente pesimista o demasiado explícita en su mensaje político. Hay algo de validez en esta crítica; la subtrama romántica entre Winston y Julia puede sentirse poco desarrollada, y el último tercio de la novela, aunque poderoso, se vuelve casi insoportablemente sombrío. Sin embargo, estos defectos parecen menores cuando se miden contra las ideas proféticas del libro.

En una era de noticias falsas, programas de vigilancia gubernamental y la erosión de la privacidad, '1984' se siente más relevante que nunca. Orwell no predijo el futuro con precisión, pero identificó los mecanismos por los cuales la libertad puede ser gradualmente desmantelada. Solo por esa razón, merece ser leído — y releído — por cada generación.

questions.question: According to the review, what is the most compelling aspect of '1984'?
options: Its detailed vision of a surveillance society.
questions.explanation: The review states that what makes the novel compelling is 'its terrifyingly detailed vision of a society built on surveillance, propaganda, and the manipulation of truth,' not the plot.

questions.question: What criticism does the reviewer acknowledge about the novel?
options: It can be seen as overly pessimistic with an underdeveloped romantic subplot.
The political message is not clear enough.
questions.explanation: The reviewer acknowledges that critics have called it 'overly pessimistic' and that 'the romantic subplot between Winston and Julia can feel underdeveloped.'

questions.question: Why does the reviewer believe '1984' is still relevant today?
options: Because of fake news, surveillance programs, and erosion of privacy.
Because the novel has been adapted into many films.
questions.explanation: The review states 'In an era of fake news, government surveillance programs, and the erosion of privacy, 1984 feels more relevant than ever.'

title: Why Do We Procrastinate?
text:
Almost everyone procrastinates from time to time, but for some people it becomes a chronic problem that affects their work, studies, and personal relationships. Understanding why we procrastinate is the first step toward overcoming it.

Contrary to popular belief, procrastination is not simply a matter of laziness or poor time management. Research in psychology suggests that it is fundamentally an emotional regulation problem. When we face a task that makes us feel anxious, bored, or overwhelmed, our brain seeks immediate relief by switching to something more pleasant — checking social media, watching videos, or reorganizing our desk for the third time.

Dr. Timothy Pychyl, a leading researcher in this field, explains that procrastination is a form of 'mood repair.' We sacrifice our long-term goals for short-term emotional comfort. The irony is that procrastination usually makes us feel worse in the long run, creating a vicious cycle of avoidance, guilt, and further avoidance.

Another factor is what psychologists call 'temporal discounting' — our tendency to value immediate rewards more highly than future ones. The satisfaction of scrolling through our phone right now feels more real and appealing than the abstract future benefit of finishing a report that is due next week.

So what can we do about it? Research suggests several evidence-based strategies. Breaking large tasks into smaller, manageable steps makes them less intimidating. Setting specific deadlines rather than vague goals creates a sense of urgency. Perhaps most importantly, practicing self-compassion — rather than criticizing ourselves for procrastinating — reduces the negative emotions that fuel the cycle.

The key insight is that overcoming procrastination is not about willpower or discipline. It is about understanding our emotions and developing healthier ways to cope with the discomfort that difficult tasks inevitably bring.
translation:
Casi todos procrastinamos de vez en cuando, pero para algunas personas se convierte en un problema crónico que afecta su trabajo, estudios y relaciones personales. Entender por qué procrastinamos es el primer paso para superarlo.

Contrariamente a la creencia popular, la procrastinación no es simplemente una cuestión de pereza o mala gestión del tiempo. La investigación en psicología sugiere que es fundamentalmente un problema de regulación emocional. Cuando enfrentamos una tarea que nos hace sentir ansiosos, aburridos o abrumados, nuestro cerebro busca alivio inmediato cambiando a algo más placentero — revisar las redes sociales, ver videos o reorganizar nuestro escritorio por tercera vez.

El Dr. Timothy Pychyl, un investigador líder en este campo, explica que la procrastinación es una forma de 'reparación del ánimo'. Sacrificamos nuestros objetivos a largo plazo por comodidad emocional a corto plazo. La ironía es que la procrastinación generalmente nos hace sentir peor a la larga, creando un ciclo vicioso de evitación, culpa y más evitación.

Otro factor es lo que los psicólogos llaman 'descuento temporal' — nuestra tendencia a valorar las recompensas inmediatas más que las futuras. La satisfacción de recorrer nuestro teléfono ahora mismo se siente más real y atractiva que el beneficio futuro abstracto de terminar un informe que hay que entregar la semana que viene.

Entonces, ¿qué podemos hacer al respecto? La investigación sugiere varias estrategias basadas en evidencia. Dividir las tareas grandes en pasos más pequeños y manejables las hace menos intimidantes. Establecer plazos específicos en lugar de metas vagas crea un sentido de urgencia. Quizás lo más importante, practicar la autocompasión — en lugar de criticarnos por procrastinar — reduce las emociones negativas que alimentan el ciclo.

La idea clave es que superar la procrastinación no se trata de fuerza de voluntad o disciplina. Se trata de entender nuestras emociones y desarrollar formas más saludables de lidiar con la incomodidad que las tareas difíciles inevitablemente traen.

questions.question: According to the text, what is the real cause of procrastination?
questions.explanation: The text states that 'Research in psychology suggests that it is fundamentally an emotional regulation problem,' contrary to the belief that it is about laziness.

questions.question: What does 'temporal discounting' mean in the context of the text?
options: Getting discounts for buying things early.
Our tendency to value immediate rewards more than future ones.
questions.explanation: The text defines temporal discounting as 'our tendency to value immediate rewards more highly than future ones.'

questions.question: Which strategy does the text suggest is 'perhaps most important' for overcoming procrastination?
questions.explanation: The text says 'Perhaps most importantly, practicing self-compassion — rather than criticizing ourselves for procrastinating — reduces the negative emotions that fuel the cycle.'

title: The Gig Economy: Freedom or Exploitation?
text:
The gig economy — a labor market characterized by short-term contracts and freelance work rather than permanent jobs — has grown dramatically in the past decade. Companies like Uber, Deliveroo, and Fiverr have created platforms that connect millions of independent workers with customers. But is this new way of working a liberation or a trap?

Advocates of the gig economy emphasize the freedom it offers. Workers can choose when, where, and how much they work. A graphic designer can take on projects from clients across the globe while sitting in a coffee shop. A driver can log in to a ride-sharing app whenever they need extra income. For people who value autonomy and flexibility above all else, the gig economy can be genuinely empowering.

Critics, however, paint a very different picture. They point out that most gig workers lack the basic protections that traditional employees enjoy: health insurance, paid sick leave, pension contributions, and unemployment benefits. The 'flexibility' that companies promote often means unpredictable income and financial insecurity. A delivery rider who earns well during peak hours might struggle during quiet periods, with no guaranteed minimum wage to fall back on.

There is also a power imbalance at the heart of the gig economy. While platforms present workers as independent contractors, these workers often have little control over crucial aspects of their work, such as pricing and customer allocation. Algorithms, not humans, make many of the decisions that determine a worker's earnings and opportunities.

Several countries are now grappling with how to regulate the gig economy. Spain has passed laws requiring platforms to treat delivery riders as employees. The UK Supreme Court ruled that Uber drivers are 'workers' entitled to minimum wage and paid holidays. These developments suggest a growing recognition that the gig economy, left unregulated, risks creating a two-tier labor market: secure, well-paid jobs for some, and precarious, underpaid work for others.

The challenge is to preserve the genuine benefits of flexible work while ensuring that the people who power these platforms are treated fairly. That will require innovative policy solutions and, above all, political will.
translation:
La economía gig — un mercado laboral caracterizado por contratos a corto plazo y trabajo freelance en lugar de empleos permanentes — ha crecido drásticamente en la última década. Empresas como Uber, Deliveroo y Fiverr han creado plataformas que conectan a millones de trabajadores independientes con clientes. Pero, ¿esta nueva forma de trabajar es una liberación o una trampa?

Los defensores de la economía gig enfatizan la libertad que ofrece. Los trabajadores pueden elegir cuándo, dónde y cuánto trabajan. Un diseñador gráfico puede tomar proyectos de clientes de todo el mundo mientras está sentado en una cafetería. Un conductor puede conectarse a una aplicación de viajes compartidos cuando necesite ingresos extra. Para las personas que valoran la autonomía y la flexibilidad por sobre todo, la economía gig puede ser genuinamente empoderadora.

Los críticos, sin embargo, pintan un cuadro muy diferente. Señalan que la mayoría de los trabajadores gig carecen de las protecciones básicas que los empleados tradicionales disfrutan: seguro médico, licencia por enfermedad paga, aportes jubilatorios y seguro de desempleo. La 'flexibilidad' que las empresas promueven a menudo significa ingresos impredecibles e inseguridad financiera. Un repartidor que gana bien durante las horas pico puede tener dificultades durante los períodos tranquilos, sin un salario mínimo garantizado como respaldo.

También hay un desequilibrio de poder en el corazón de la economía gig. Si bien las plataformas presentan a los trabajadores como contratistas independientes, estos trabajadores a menudo tienen poco control sobre aspectos cruciales de su trabajo, como los precios y la asignación de clientes. Los algoritmos, no los humanos, toman muchas de las decisiones que determinan las ganancias y oportunidades de un trabajador.

Varios países están ahora lidiando con cómo regular la economía gig. España ha aprobado leyes que requieren que las plataformas traten a los repartidores como empleados. La Corte Suprema del Reino Unido dictaminó que los conductores de Uber son 'trabajadores' con derecho a salario mínimo y vacaciones pagas. Estos desarrollos sugieren un creciente reconocimiento de que la economía gig, sin regulación, corre el riesgo de crear un mercado laboral de dos niveles: empleos seguros y bien pagos para algunos, y trabajo precario y mal pago para otros.

El desafío es preservar los beneficios genuinos del trabajo flexible mientras se asegura que las personas que impulsan estas plataformas sean tratadas justamente. Eso requerirá soluciones políticas innovadoras y, sobre todo, voluntad política.

questions.question: What benefit of the gig economy do its advocates emphasize?
options: The freedom to choose when, where, and how much to work.
questions.explanation: The text says advocates 'emphasize the freedom it offers. Workers can choose when, where, and how much they work.'

questions.question: What does the text identify as a fundamental problem with the gig economy's power structure?
options: Customers have too much power over workers.
Workers set their own prices too high.
Algorithms, not humans, control key decisions affecting workers' earnings.
Gig workers earn more than traditional employees.
questions.explanation: The text states that 'Algorithms, not humans, make many of the decisions that determine a worker's earnings and opportunities.'

questions.question: What did the UK Supreme Court decide about Uber drivers?
options: They are independent contractors with no special rights.
They are full employees of Uber.
They are 'workers' entitled to minimum wage and paid holidays.
They must form a labor union.
questions.explanation: The text says 'The UK Supreme Court ruled that Uber drivers are workers entitled to minimum wage and paid holidays.'

title: A Letter from Abroad
text:
Dear Professor Martínez,

I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to share an update on my experience as an exchange student at the University of Edinburgh, as you kindly asked me to do before I left.

I have been here for nearly four months now, and I must say that the experience has exceeded my expectations in many ways. The academic environment is stimulating and rigorous. The courses I am taking — Contemporary British Literature, Sociolinguistics, and Research Methodology — have challenged me to think critically in ways I hadn't anticipated. The professors encourage debate and independent thought, and the assessment system relies heavily on essays and presentations rather than written exams, which I have found both liberating and demanding.

Beyond academics, life in Edinburgh has been an education in itself. The city is breathtaking, with its medieval Old Town and elegant Georgian New Town. I have attended the tail end of the Edinburgh Festival, where I saw theatre productions and comedy shows from around the world. The cultural diversity here is remarkable — my flatmates come from Nigeria, South Korea, and Sweden, and our evening conversations have broadened my perspective more than any textbook could.

I will not pretend that everything has been easy. The Scottish weather takes some getting used to, and there have been moments of homesickness that caught me off guard. The cost of living is considerably higher than at home, which has forced me to budget carefully. I have also had to adapt to a very different teaching style, where students are expected to do extensive independent reading rather than relying primarily on lectures.

Nevertheless, I would not trade this experience for anything. It has strengthened my English, deepened my academic interests, and given me a confidence that I did not possess before. I look forward to discussing my research project with you upon my return in June.

With warm regards,
Lucía Fernández
translation:
Estimado Profesor Martínez,

Espero que esta carta lo encuentre bien. Le escribo para compartir una actualización sobre mi experiencia como estudiante de intercambio en la Universidad de Edimburgo, como amablemente me pidió que hiciera antes de irme.

Llevo aquí casi cuatro meses, y debo decir que la experiencia ha superado mis expectativas en muchos sentidos. El ambiente académico es estimulante y riguroso. Los cursos que estoy tomando — Literatura Británica Contemporánea, Sociolingüística y Metodología de Investigación — me han desafiado a pensar críticamente de maneras que no había anticipado. Los profesores fomentan el debate y el pensamiento independiente, y el sistema de evaluación depende en gran medida de ensayos y presentaciones en lugar de exámenes escritos, lo cual me ha parecido tanto liberador como exigente.

Más allá de lo académico, la vida en Edimburgo ha sido una educación en sí misma. La ciudad es impresionante, con su Ciudad Vieja medieval y su elegante Ciudad Nueva georgiana. Asistí al final del Festival de Edimburgo, donde vi producciones teatrales y shows de comedia de todo el mundo. La diversidad cultural aquí es notable — mis compañeros de departamento son de Nigeria, Corea del Sur y Suecia, y nuestras conversaciones nocturnas han ampliado mi perspectiva más de lo que cualquier libro de texto podría.

No voy a pretender que todo ha sido fácil. El clima escocés requiere cierta adaptación, y ha habido momentos de nostalgia que me tomaron desprevenida. El costo de vida es considerablemente más alto que en casa, lo que me ha obligado a presupuestar cuidadosamente. También tuve que adaptarme a un estilo de enseñanza muy diferente, donde se espera que los estudiantes hagan lectura independiente extensa en lugar de depender principalmente de las clases.

Sin embargo, no cambiaría esta experiencia por nada. Ha fortalecido mi inglés, profundizado mis intereses académicos y me ha dado una confianza que no poseía antes. Espero con ansias discutir mi proyecto de investigación con usted a mi regreso en junio.

Con cálidos saludos,
Lucía Fernández

questions.question: How does the assessment system at Edinburgh differ from what Lucía is used to?
options: It focuses on essays and presentations rather than written exams.
There are no assessments at all.
questions.explanation: Lucía says 'the assessment system relies heavily on essays and presentations rather than written exams.'

questions.question: What aspect of Edinburgh life broadened Lucía's perspective the most?
options: Evening conversations with flatmates from different countries.
questions.explanation: Lucía says her evening conversations with flatmates from Nigeria, South Korea, and Sweden 'have broadened my perspective more than any textbook could.'

questions.question: What teaching style difference did Lucía have to adapt to?
options: Students must do extensive independent reading instead of relying on lectures.
All courses are taught in Scottish dialect.
There are no professors, only tutors.
questions.explanation: Lucía mentions adapting to a style 'where students are expected to do extensive independent reading rather than relying primarily on lectures.'
